text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic intracorporeal anastomosis between the transverse colon and descending colon. The transverse colon is positioned down to the descending colon sigma junction area. A stay stitch is placed to assess the alignment of the anastomosis, followed by the insertion of a stapler. Entratomies are made on both the transverse and descending colon.

The surgeon uses an L hook to create precise enterotomies on the colon, opening them along the tinea coli for anastomosis. A blue load stapler is used to join the soft colon tissue, with the stapler fitting adequately within the created openings.

The surgeon closes the defect using a stapler, followed by placing three sutures to reinforce the staple lines on the colon. The video shows the stapling already performed, with the staple lines reinforced with limbered sutures.

This doesn't usually take much time, which adds extra ten minutes to the operative procedure, but gives me the peace that the stapling is reinforced. Theoretically, this is not required. If the stapling all went well, you really don't need to, but given the advantage of laparoscopy and suturing, it just gives me a feel that the entire stapling is nicely reinforced. Taking the idea that we have in the open surgery where we reinforce the staple line adequately. Here there are two staple lines next to each other. That is a concern for some surgeons but we have seen in multiple gastrointestinal procedures that the two staple lines close to each other does not cause any ischemia to the bowel itself.

But definitely reinforcing the staple line with a suture gives you the peace of mind at night as a surgeon so I ended up choosing to close this suture line. The patient did very well after surgery and was discharged home three days after surgery itself with a return of bowel function quickly. The specimen was removed through a fan still incision that also helped with the pain. There were more than twelve lymph nodes retrieved in the specimen.
Thank you for watching. Please like and share
